dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for unconscious/fainting/near-fainting call at --Address Redacted--.
Hi, I'm Jordan, an EMT with the rescue squad. Would you or your friend mind stepping back a bit so we can assess her?
Her name is Sheila. She fainted after slipping in the mud, hit her head here. She was out for 2-3 minutes.
Thank you, we're going to take good care of her. Sheila, if you can hear me, can you tell me your name?
S… She...la? I don't know what happened.
That's okay, Sheila. We're with you. Can you look at my badge for me?
Eyes are opening. I see them tracking the light.
Sheila, can I lift your arm for a second?
Huh? Yeah, fine.
Great, everything seems cooperative for now.
Sheila, we're going to make sure your airway is clear. Can I inspect your mouth?
Airway is clear and not compromised. No signs of bleeding.
She's breathing on her own and not struggling.
No swelling or deformity observed in the neck or face.
Respiratory rate appears to be around 20 per minute, no signs of difficulty.
Breath sounds on both sides are clear and symmetrical.
Oxygen saturation is 100%. No signs of hypoxia.
Pulse is 89, strong and regular.
Her skin is dry and warm with normal coloration.
Peripheral pulses-radial and pedal-are strong and regular.
Can you tell me about Sheila's medical history?
What medications or allergies should we be aware of?
She had cancer, but it's been in remission for five years now. She had some ETOH tonight, can't recall how much.
When was the last time she ate or drank something?
At a bar earlier tonight. She had a drink, but doesn't remember the amount.
Sheila, can you tell us what you remember about the fall?
I don't know. I remember being inside the bar.
Are you experiencing any pain or discomfort right now?
No pain at all. Just confused about what happened.
Partner, check the skin for signs of injury or swelling, particularly on the head.
There's some swelling in the left occipital area with a minor amount of bleeding noted. No abrasions here.
Let's assess her neurologic function. Sheila, it's bright in here, can you tell me what day it is?
I... don't remember. Thursday?
She's opening her eyes and responding. She follows commands appropriately, even if her memory is compromised.
GCS score is 14-E4, V4, and M6. Indicating decreased alertness but no severe neurological deficits at this point.
Pupils are equal and round with some delayed reaction, but still reactive.
No signs of nuchal rigidity or tenderness in the neck area.
Let's listen for abnormal breath sounds. Both sides are equal now.
Lungs are clear bilaterally with no wheezes or crackles.
No chest deformities observed, and the trachea is centered.
Pulse is 89, strong and regular.
Respiratory rate is steady at 20-within normal limits.
Blood pressure is 138 over 83. All vitals appear to be within the normal range for her.
Blood glucose level is 87 mg/dL-normal and no urgent evidence of hypoglycemia.
SpO2 is 100%, so oxygenation is currently adequate.
Partner, we need to stabilize her cervical spine manually.
Cervical spine has been manually aligned with no force applied-she shows no resistance.
Apply a cervical collar to maintain spinal immobilization.
Cervical collar is on and positioned to keep the spine neutral.
No signs of discomfort or resistance after collar application.
Pulse now reads 84. Stable at this time.
Blood pressure is now 160 over 98. Mildly elevated but within normal for trauma patients.
Begin establishing intravenous access before transport using a large-bore needle.
18G IV access successfully placed at left antecubital vein. Saline lock is secured.
Apply the cardiac monitor to detect any arrhythmias during transport.
Four-lead EKG is connected and reflecting sinus rhythm-normal.
Glucometer shows 0. Will retest or check if equipment is working properly.
SpO2 at 98%-slight drop, but still acceptable.
EKG continues to show sinus rhythm. No new electrical abnormalities observed.
Initiate the general trauma management protocol due to head injury and altered mental status.
Cervical spine precautions in place. Follow trauma guidelines for patients with AMS.
Pulse now 84-slightly decreased from earlier reading.
Blood pressure remains at 160 over 98 initially.
SpO2 is down to 98%, but that is well within the acceptable range.
Sheila, are there any sharp or new pains we should be checking?
No-just confusion. No pain felt anywhere.
Sheila, during all our assessments, is there anything fuzzy or difficult that you're finding hard to process now?
I have trouble remembering-keeps coming up blank, but nothing new.
Have you experienced any weakness or trouble moving any part of your body since the fall?
No. I feel good there. I can move my arms and legs fine.
Let's test your motor and sensory function again. Can you move each finger and toe one by one?
Movement appears intact and vigorous in hands, feet, and all four extremities.
She has full range of motion in her shoulders, hips, arms, and legs.
Securing for transport to [COMPANY_NAME]-your nearest trauma center.
[COMPANY_NAME] can treat head injuries and AMS with CT and specialist support.
She is ready for safe transfer. Prepare for stretcher loading.
Sheila, we're gently placing you onto the stretcher now. Just relax and trust us.
She's secured with five-point harnessing and placed in a comfortable position.
A blanket has been added for comfort and kept loose for breathability.
Loading her into the ambulance. Stay in communication with the crew during transport.
Blood pressure is now 155 over 97-now showing slight elevation.
Pulse is now 91-increased slightly compared to the baseline.
SpO2 is 99. Remaining within acceptable oxygenation limits.
Completed another round of pain reassessment-still a 0 score.
She has been loaded in the back. Transport crew will monitor throughout the ride.
She is safely secured and ready for transport.
All equipment in place and ready. Doors are closed and locked.
Ready for en route monitoring and continuous reassessment during transport.
Blood pressure down slightly-150 over 95 now.
Pulse is back to 88-now within the normal range.
SpO2 is still at 99%-consistent and well-oxygenated.
Have you had any additional trouble with orientation or memory while we're en route?
I keep forgetting. Only that frayed edge of memory.
Partner, let's check again if she has intact motor and sensory control.
Motor and sensory responses in all extremities are intact.
Range of motion remains full and controlled across all limbs.
Arrival at [COMPANY_NAME] is expected within 5 minutes.
Retest equipment as we move so we can respond rapidly if necessary.
Ahead to the hospital and report current vitals and condition in detail.
She is now being transferred into the trauma room at [COMPANY_NAME].
